questions.question: Define a Turing Machine and explain its components.
keyPoints: Turing Machine is an abstract computational model by Alan Turing (1936)
Components: Infinite tape divided into cells
Read/Write head that moves left/right along tape
Finite control (state register)
Transition function/table of instructions
Finite alphabet including input symbols and blank symbol

questions.question: Explain the Church-Turing Thesis with its significance.
keyPoints: States that any computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine
Established by Alonzo Church and Alan Turing independently
Provides a formal definition of 'computable'
Serves as the gold standard for comparing computational models
Has withstood extensive testing and acceptance in CS theory

questions.question: Differentiate between Recursively Enumerable (RE) and Recursive languages.
keyPoints: RE: There exists a TM that accepts all strings in L (may not halt on strings not in L)
Recursive: There exists a TM that always halts, accepting strings in L and rejecting strings not in L
Recursive languages are a proper subset of RE
Example: Halting Problem language is RE but not recursive (undecidable)

questions.question: What is a Universal Turing Machine? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Universal Turing Machine can simulate any other Turing Machine
Takes as input: encoding of any TM + input for that TM
Important as foundation for stored-program computers
Conceptually equivalent to modern general-purpose computers
Demonstrates that one machine can perform any computational task given proper input

questions.question: Explain the Halting Problem and its implications.
keyPoints: Problem: Given any program and input, determine if program halts
Turing proved it is undecidable (cannot be solved by any algorithm)
Has profound implications: Some problems have no algorithmic solution
Leads to limits of what computers can compute
Foundation for computability theory and computational limits

questions.question: List and explain the elements of Turing Machine's 7-tuple representation.
keyPoints: Q: Finite set of states
Σ: Input alphabet (excluding blank)
Γ: Tape alphabet (includes Σ and blank B)
δ: Transition function (Q × Γ → Q × Γ × {L,R})
q0: Initial/starting state (q0 ∈ Q)
B: Blank symbol (B ∈ Γ - Σ)
F: Set of final/accepting states (F ⊆ Q)

questions.question: Compare Deterministic and Non-Deterministic Turing Machines.
keyPoints: DTM: Each (state, symbol) pair has at most one transition
NDTM: Each (state, symbol) pair can have multiple transitions
NDTM accepts if some sequence of choices leads to acceptance
Both accept exactly the same class of languages (recursively enumerable)
NDTM can be simulated by DTM (difference in time complexity for bounded variants)

questions.question: Define the Halting Problem. Provide Turing's proof of its undecidability. What are the practical implications of this result?
keyPoints: Definition: Given any program P and input I, determine if P(I) halts
Proof outline: Assume a decider H(P,I) exists that solves Halting Problem
Construct paradox: Machine D that calls H on itself with its own description
D(D) halts iff D(D) doesn't halt - contradiction
Therefore H cannot exist - Halting Problem is undecidable
Implications: No general algorithm can analyze arbitrary programs; compiler optimizations limited; software verification has fundamental bounds; proves existence of uncomputable functions

questions.question: Explain Turing Machine as a language recognizer and as a computing device with suitable examples. How does it differ from Finite Automata?
keyPoints: As Language Recognizer: TM reads input, moves through configurations, accepts if enters final state, may reject or loop forever
As Computing Device: TM computes functions - writes output on tape and halts (e.g., adding numbers)
Difference from FA: TM has infinite tape memory, can move both directions, can rewrite tape symbols
FA: finite memory, only accepts/recognizes regular languages
TM: can recognize context-sensitive (CSL) and type-0 languages
TM can simulate any FA (can implement finite control transitions)
Power difference due to unbounded tape serving as memory

questions.question: Explain the concept of Universal Turing Machine and its significance in the theory of computation. How does it relate to modern computers?
keyPoints: Universal Turing Machine (UTM): A TM that can simulate any other TM
Input to UTM: Encoding of any TM M (as string) + encoding of input w for M
UTM simulates M's behavior on w by interpreting M's transition rules
Significance: Proves single machine can perform any algorithmic task
Foundation of stored-program concept: program and data both stored on tape/in memory
Modern computers are practical implementations of UTM concept
CPU = finite control, Memory = tape, Software = encoding of simulated TM
Establishes theoretical limits: If not computable by TM, not computable by any real computer

questions.question: Discuss various Turing Machine variations (multi-tape, multi-head, non-deterministic) and prove that they are equivalent in computational power to the basic Turing Machine.
keyPoints: Multi-tape TM: Multiple tapes with independent heads - can simulate single-tape TM
Multi-head TM: Multiple read/write heads on single tape - can simulate single-head TM
Non-deterministic TM (NDTM): Multiple transition choices - can be simulated by DTM
Equivalence proof: Show each variant can simulate basic TM and vice versa
Basic TM can simulate multi-tape: Encode multiple tapes on single tape with delimiters
Basic TM can simulate NDTM: Try all branches sequentially using systematic search (breadth-first)
All variants accept exactly recursively enumerable languages
Different only in time complexity, not language class
